chunk_summary: Guidelines for allergen introduction in former preterm infants emphasize developmental readiness, medical stability, and a cautious approach for high-risk cases, ensuring allergens are introduced without compromising core nutrition or milk intake.
learning_objectives: Understand the developmental and medical readiness criteria for allergen introduction in former preterm infants.
Identify high-risk infants requiring supervised allergen introduction and appropriate clinical consultation.
Learn practical methods for introducing common allergens like peanut and egg in safe, age-appropriate forms.
Recognize the importance of integrating allergen introduction into balanced complementary feeding without displacing core nutrition.
key_facts: Delaying allergenic food introduction does not prevent allergies and may increase caregiver anxiety.
Introduce allergens when the infant has good head control, can sit with support, and safely take thick purees.
High-risk infants (e.g., severe eczema, known food allergy, strong family history) may require supervised allergen introduction.
Common first allergens include cooked egg and peanut in safe forms (e.g., thin peanut paste mixed into puree).
Observe for immediate symptoms (hives, vomiting, wheeze) and delayed symptoms (eczema flare, blood in stool) after allergen introduction.
Infants with cow’s milk protein allergy (CMPA) should not be tested with cow’s milk at home unless clinically supervised.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Delaying allergenic foods does not prevent allergy and may increase caregiver anxiety and unnecessary restriction. (NIAID Addendum Guidelines)
document_key_topics: Developmental and medical readiness for complementary feeding
Integration of allergens into core nutrition
